speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's the 18th of February, 1996. The survivor is Sarah Brodaty. The interviewer is Libby Raichman. The city is Sydney. We're in New South Wales, Australia. And the language today is English. OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Sunday, February the 18th, 1996. I'm interviewing Sarah Getz-- Sarah Brodaty. I'm Libby Raichman. I'm interviewing her in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah, can you please tell me we you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am born in Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me where about in Poland Luck is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not far from the-- used to be not far from the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. What did the town look like? Was it a pretty town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very pretty town, about 32,000 population. A lot of Jews were living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you know what the population of Jewish people was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about 3,000 or more. I-- I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a river in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a river, Styr. Yes. It was around town, going. It was following the Bug, the river, Bug, where--

speaker: interviewer
text: It joined the river, Bug.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, false ending, join, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just a small-- a smaller river?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not the-- it wasn't small. It was quite big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Bug was bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And did you swim in the river as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did people swim in the river?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very big river.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there-- were there a lot of trees in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it green?

speaker: interviewee
text: Green, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And parks? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very many parks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people go on picnics around the river and so on? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I can't remember. I know only near Luck was a-- a holiday resort, [POLISH]. And there, we used to go. It was a-- a forest. It was very nice there. And there-- there [POLISH] was a station. What-- what comes from Russia, and changed. Because the line was narrower in Russia. And in Poland was wider. So they changed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Like a junction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a junction. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah, tell me about your family. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, whatever you remember. Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was always busy and-- with charity-- not charity, how to say? He was a manager for the-- for the-- for people. He was the director. And my-- my mother used to be, all the time, in the shop. We had a shoe shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was Chana Leah. And my father was Elkana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Getz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getz. And your parents had a shoe shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shoe shop. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father not work in the shoe shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked from-- when he has time. He worked when he-- he wasn't busy with the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, he was busy with the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish community? Or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish community, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was-- was he involved with the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very much involved at the shul. He was the-- the main director in the shul. He wasn't a [YIDDISH]. He was just really-- a very religious person. His father was a rabbi. Not in Luck, in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was in love with a woman. And my-- my grandfather said, while I'm alive, you're not going to marry her. So he came to us to visit, one Friday. And Friday, in the evening, people take [NON-ENGLISH] for their-- for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: A visitor?

speaker: interviewee
text: A visitor. So he-- they took him. My grandfather took him. And he fell in love with my mother. And he married her.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. That's quite a sweet story. And-- and your father remained in Luck after that, all the years?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the year, he remained in Luck. He died in, I think, in  '42. I am not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1942, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who opened the shoe shop that your parents had?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father used to open it. And then my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who began the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the Auntie Perl, from America. She gave it to-- to my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when she left to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- how was she related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when your mother's sister left and emigrated to America, she handed over the shop to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father and your mother worked in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But your father was obviously involved with communal activities.

speaker: interviewee
text: With communal, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did he do, Sarah? Besides the shul and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And communal? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But he was involved with the people in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very much involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they buy the shoes, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to go to Lodz, to Warsaw. That's what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there was the-- the main.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the shop as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd have been only a child. When my father died, I was-- I was six  years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So your father-- so your father didn't-- you said, your father died in 1942. But it must have been much earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were six years old, your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '20, 1920. I'll have been 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was 1932, not--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, '42. I think most probably, 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were born in 1920--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- and your-- and you were 12 years old when your father died--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you were-- there he died in 1932, before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he died before the war. A lot. A lot before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So that was 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the war started in '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where-- so where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. You're right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so when did he die, Sarah? In--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have-- were you at school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to a-- a public school. And-- and my sister from America, she-- when my father died, she took care of-- of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: While your mother worked in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's what I remember. And do you remember what you learned at school? What subjects your learned? In what language were you taught?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything in Polish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your home language, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke Yiddish at home, and Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: At school. Did you learn any other languages at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and when you went to high school, what languages did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did all general subjects and German?

speaker: interviewee
text: German, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Sarah, tell me about your parents' home. Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living, actually, in-- in a flat, what belonged to my auntie. She give it to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same auntie who went to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Auntie Perl?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, another auntie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a big yard. It was a lot of flats. And they'd been living in one of the houses, and there were others [? like it. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there with your mother and father when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but before that, you were living there with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: All my family. There were sisters and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All, we lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What sisters and brothers did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters and one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names? What was your oldest sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister's name was Zlate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then was Gittel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother was Shmulik and then me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you. And Sarah, did any of them leave Poland before the War?

speaker: interviewee
text: One month before the war, my brother left. And my sister left in 1935, to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: She went to America. Where did your brother go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they both married when they left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. My brother was single.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister married in Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was married in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Luck, for American boy who came to-- to the Yeshiva. We had the Yeshiva. His mother sent him to the Yeshiva. And he came to give it-- my auntie came from America this year, 1935. And he came to send regards to his parents. And he saw my sister and fell in love with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took her back to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they married and took her back to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: They married in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Luck, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took her back to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother went to Israel. He was still single?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was single. He went with-- from the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Was he involved in Jewish youth movements?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He was a [? Shomer ?] [? Yisrael. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he settle in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went kibbutz-- on the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the-- the-- the-- the English-- took-- they ran away to-- to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cyprus. And then he went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time when the British were--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the British were in Palestine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were occupying Palestine. And your-- and your sister, Gittel, what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she was the one who went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister was married already. She was-- she stays in Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. She married somebody from Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: She married somebody from Luck, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had children together?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they're now in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And Sarah, your own youth, you-- when you were growing up, were-- how did you study, when you left school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I study, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn-- what did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me think. I went to-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I study a-- the nursing course. Yes. Was there a special school for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a school, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: For-- for nursing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a evening course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked? No. I didn't work in the hospital. I just finish, and the war start. And I run to-- to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Now, I want to know about your life in Luck. I mean, there were non-Jewish people in Luck, besides the-- the Jewish community. Did you have any contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In school, I went to a Polish public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a German girlfriend from Czechoslovakia, from Czech-- a Czech girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were friendly with non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much friendly, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got on well with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any-- as the war came closer, were there any acts of anti-Semitism in your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went from that school to another school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a Jewish public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because they throw me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they throw you out? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been a very big [NON-ENGLISH]. How you say [NON-ENGLISH]? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: A loafer. Were-- were you a naughty girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, very naughty, very. As they used to say, I am like a village girl. I had the red cheeks and white, completely white hair. And everybody asked my mother, from where you got that? From the milkman? [LAUGHTER] And we decided to paint the black boat with grease, oil, and-- and then they find out that I'd been the-- the main organizer, of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So they--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they throw me out and I went to Jewish public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So Sarah, do you remember there being any anti-Semitism in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Ukraine. Because that used to belong to Ukraine, Luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Ukraine gym-- high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, where we live, in the top, it was-- the high school. And they-- they shout at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shout at us, the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- You're Jewish! You're Jewish! You're-- they'd do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to shout it at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they say? Shout in Russian, of course?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And besides that, there were no special incidence in your life, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I-- that-- that-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak fluent German, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I understand German. But I can't speak fluent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was your family religious, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: They kept a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Kosher, and special for Pesach, for [INAUDIBLE] there was a special [INAUDIBLE] dishes, was standing in-- in-- in-- at-- at our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did your family go to shul often, to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very often. My father belongs to [? it ?] [? then. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go yourself, with children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother used to go. I don't-- I went from time to time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Sarah, tell me about-- about it, when-- and when Hitler came into-- became the Chancellor of Germany, in 1933, did that affect your life in Poland at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler didn't came in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not to you. He came in-- your--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We didn't-- we knew about it. But we didn't feel it yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: People are talking, came in from Russia, or came in from other towns. They know. They told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened as the war got closer? What did you hear about, what was going on in Germany? Did you hear anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were quite protected from what--

speaker: interviewee
text: From what are going on. For-- for-- for this [? time. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Till they came to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, when did-- when do you remember the-- the beginning of the war? What did you remember about the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in one night, we hear shooting. I don't remember what date was it. And we knew, already, the war, they're coming. The Germans are coming. Because first came the Russian to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few years, the Germans come. And soon the Russian go-- went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the Germans came into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: They start to-- not to Poland. To our town. Poland was a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: The time before. OK, when did they come to Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Luck? They come in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in '42 or '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were already 21, 22 at that time. And were you working as a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I had been working in a shop before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Just before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war. Just before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't practice your nursing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't practice my nursing, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what-- were you married yet, or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were single?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, single.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when the Germans came in--

speaker: interviewee
text: They start to bomb-- bomb?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bomb.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bomb the town. And I've been, already, married. I married, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had been pregnant. And I-- what I remember, we were in-- it's-- [? in a house ?] in-- in the basement, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding. And I remember, we went out. When the bombing stopped, we went out. And now we're standing in the middle of this-- of the road. And I thought, I will see where the houses will fall. So I will run in the other side. That's what I remember, distinctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to see where the bombing took place, so that you could go and find shelter somewhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Our house was a new one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it remained standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remained. Still standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to this day?

speaker: interviewee
text: To this day. Because it was-- that was my auntie's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three floor, with shops in the front. And they didn't allow-- because of the war-- they [? thought ?] they make a-- an iron roof with cement.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the war stop-- when the bombing stopped-- and people-- we left already. And the people went on the roof. The bombs was lying in the-- in the [? seam ?] of the roof. It didn't explode.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your roof?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because-- because it was made with a--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. But because made--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just [? reinforced ?] steel girders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Steel, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really? All right, so now when the-- when the-- when the bombing was over on that first time, what did you do, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: We prepared to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is we?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and a lot of people. The Russian trucks came from the front. In-- in our house was living a Russian high-rank officers or what-- whatever. He went in the army and the wife and his children were there. So he get-- the truck came to pick them up. And we went on the truck too.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this Russian officer's wife was living in your building--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the war time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians-- when the Russians occupied.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians came on. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so the truck came to pick up his wife and children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went with, on the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who else went? You and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband. And my mother was standing with a basket, to-- to put her on. And they run away. So they left here. And I'd been nine months pregnant. And my husband looked. And he couldn't find me. People were standing on me, on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were standing on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: On me. That's because they throw me on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so your one sister was in America. And your brother was in Israel. Your father had already died. And-- and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was with me, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older sister was with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: She and her-- she on her own?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband, and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did she have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was their family, and you and your husband. And you were pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was leaving behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't she get onto the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody put her up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never found her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never find her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with her afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the last time you saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the last time I saw her. I [? can't ?] [? see ?] [? her. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah, what did you take with you that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I took?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been wearing a morning gown.

speaker: interviewer
text: A-- a gown?

speaker: interviewee
text: A gown. Not a dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what-- and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, has-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- a raining coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and dressed underneath?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. dressed underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't even have a change of clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sister? Did she manage to take anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I can't tell. I don't remember. But probably she did, because the children were there. The children were small children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And where did you go, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us-- the truck took us to the Russian border. And they left us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the place that was on the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I just remember that my-- my brother-in-law left the suit-- their hat back. And he has money in that attache case. And my-- my husband went to look for that truck. And that was in the evening, in the afternoon. It was, till seven o'clock, you can walk around. And [? there it ?] was past seven o'clock. He's start to run. And a little girl said, he's a-- he's a spy. He's a spy, [INAUDIBLE], because he wears-- wear a white rain coat that looks like he's a spy. So they took him in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the-- there, at the Russian border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This still belongs to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, there were-- were mean the Russian-occupied part of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans used to come after them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you came to this-- to this place. And your brother-in-law, remember that he left his attache case on the-- on the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband went to look for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they arrested him?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They arrested him. And he was sleeping overnight there. And it's not a jail. It's-- I don't know what it was. And in the morning, I went there. And I start to cry. But because the Germans came, the Russians moved away and left everything. They run away and they left everything. And I went there to find the passport and to find my husband. And we start to run to-- to Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your sister with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and my brother-in-law and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went from the Polish-Russian border--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you crossed over, traveling eastwards to Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- what did you do? You were still in a gown, Sarah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have relatives there, in Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You arrived in Kiev in a gown.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came to your relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to my relatives. They give me something to wear. And from there, we run away. Because the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, with this time, how long were you with your relatives in Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not long. Maybe one day or two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: All-- all six of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All six of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The four of them and the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there we were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You separated from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From my sister and my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my-- my brother-in-law wasn't very fair to me. And my husband didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened between you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have what to eat. Whatever he have something, he find something to eat, he give the children. And I'd been pregnant, he didn't give me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband said, oh-- oh, your sister or me. So I have to go with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they survived? Did that sister survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- OK. All right, so what happened? That you-- you left them in Kiev? And you-- where did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We went-- we ran from Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: All six of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All six, in different directions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Khar'kov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they went, I think to Dzambul, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how long were you in Khar'kov?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Khar'kov, I give birth to my girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- what was her-- what did you call her, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bronye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Were you in a hospital when you had her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They give me to the hospital. And my-- at least I have something to eat. And my husband was running around, looking in the window, to see what I am doing. And somebody took him in, a Russian man, Russian family. And they feed him. And we'd been in Khar'kov not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you call not long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, was it like, a week, a month?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a month. Not a week. A few days. And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there just long enough to have the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby. And from there, we run to Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you get from Kiev to Khar'kov to Krasnodar? How did you get from the one to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a-- a train. I think so, I'm not sure. With the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: On kettle-- on kettle trucks? Or-- or normal trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember only a kettle truck we went from Russia, when the war stopped-- finished, we went to Poland, it was a kettle.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. When you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in between, did you walk any of those distances? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember we walk-- in-- in the field from-- from wheat. And my-- the-- my grand-- my niece-- the-- the German went down and shot-- shot us. And we-- she-- we went in the wheat to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't find you there, obviously.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they'd been in the plane.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. They were shooting you from above.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked through fields.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my niece was-- she was a little girl. And she was screaming and-- terrifying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we run from place to place. OK, so then you-- you-- you left Khar'kov. And you went to-- can you just repeat the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Krasnodar. How long were you in Krasnodar?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krasnodar, we'd been a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there, Sarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband went in the field to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the-- for the-- for the [NON-ENGLISH], for the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why they feed him. And I had been sitting in place with a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which place? Where were you, with the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Krasnodar.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- in a village, in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody--

speaker: interviewee
text: In somebody's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I'd-- I'd been starving from hunger. And my child starve from hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the people give you any food? The people that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give me food, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to rely on your husband to bring food home for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he's at the field, eating. And I had been starving with my child in there-- there, inside, in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of interview with Sarah Brodaty. It's the 18th of February, 1996. And we are in Sydney, Australia.
